# A Systematic Review of Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction for Chronic Low Back Pain

## Methods
We conducted a systematic review following PRISMA 2020 reporting guidance. Eligible studies were
randomized or registered controlled trials of mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) in adults
with chronic low back pain (CLBP) reporting pain, physical function, or disability outcomes. Two
reviewers independently screened records, extracted data, and resolved disagreements by consensus.

## Search Strategy
We searched PubMed and Embase from 2010-01-01 to 2024-12-31. The full query string was:
`("mindfulness" OR "MBSR") AND ("low back pain" OR "lumbar") AND (trial OR randomized)`. No
language restriction was applied, and the reference lists of included studies were hand-searched.

## PRISMA Flow
1,204 records were identified through database searching. After de-duplication, 980 records were
screened on title and abstract, and 112 full texts were assessed for eligibility. 70 reports were
excluded with reasons (wrong population, n=31; no MBSR arm, n=24; no eligible outcome, n=15). 42
studies were included in the qualitative synthesis.

## Results
Across the included trials, mindfulness-based stress reduction reduced pain interference at 26
weeks compared with usual care [S1]. An 8-week mindfulness program improved physical function
scores relative to a waitlist control [S2], and mindfulness-based stress reduction lowered
self-reported pain intensity versus usual care [S3]. Mindfulness participants reported fewer days
of restricted activity than the education control group at six months [S4]. Adherence to the
mindfulness sessions was associated with greater improvement in disability scores [S5]. No
significant difference in opioid use between mindfulness and usual care was found at 12 months
[S6].

Risk of bias was assessed for each included study using the Cochrane risk-of-bias tool; most
trials were at low or moderate risk, with performance bias the most common concern given the
unblindable nature of the intervention.

## Discussion
The evidence indicates a small-to-moderate benefit of MBSR on pain interference and physical
function in CLBP, consistent across the included primary trials.

### Limitations
Heterogeneity in outcome measures and follow-up windows limited pooling, most trials could not
blind participants, and long-term (>12 month) data were sparse. Publication bias could not be
excluded.

## References
- [S1] Randomized trial of MBSR for chronic low back pain (peer-reviewed-primary-study).
- [S2] Registered 8-week mindfulness program trial (registered-trial-report).
- [S3] Pooled primary studies of MBSR pain intensity (peer-reviewed-primary-study).
- [S4] Preregistered MBSR restricted-activity study (preregistration-record).
- [S5] Primary trial of adherence and disability outcomes (peer-reviewed-primary-study).
- [S6] Registered report on opioid use (registered-trial-report).
